signals: Guidelines are provided for the initial management of eight incidentalomas
pituitary-specific magnetic resonance imaging if the lesion is 1 cm or larger
nodules larger than 1 to 2 cm are of greater concern
pulmonary incidentalomas larger than 8 mm or those with irregular borders
The Bosniak classification is a well-accepted means of triaging renal incidentalomas

extraction.summary: This guideline addresses the initial management of eight types of incidental findings (incidentalomas) discovered on imaging studies: pituitary, thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic, pancreatic, adrenal, renal, and ovarian lesions. The primary approach focuses on size-based thresholds and imaging characteristics to determine which lesions require further workup versus monitoring.

population.age_criteria: Variable by organ system - reproductive age women for hepatic adenomas, older adults for renal cysts (up to one-third), any age for other incidentalomas
population.risk_criteria: Risk factors include smoking history, asbestos exposure, family history, history of malignancy, radiation exposure (thyroid), oral contraceptive use (hepatic adenomas)
population.disease_definition: Incidental findings on radiologic studies that are of unclear clinical significance, most of which prove to be benign
population.exclusions: Patients with active cancer or obvious metastatic disease require different management algorithms
population.severity_staging: Size-based classification systems including Bosniak classification for renal lesions

key_thresholds.context: Threshold for MRI with pituitary protocol
Cutoff below which no further imaging warranted
Threshold for concern and further workup
Low attenuation threshold for benign lesions
Surgery recommended for cysts larger than this with suspicious features
Threshold for lipid-rich adenoma on unenhanced CT
Complex cysts larger than this require follow-up
Benign cysts smaller than this do not require follow-up
Benign cysts smaller than this do not require follow-up

recommendations.recommendation: MRI with pituitary protocol for lesions ≥1 cm or abutting optic chiasm
Follow thyroid nodules ≤1 cm with ultrasonography in 6 months
No further imaging for pulmonary nodules ≤4 mm in low-risk patients
Multiphasic CT or MRI preferred for hepatic lesion characterization
Monitor all pancreatic cystic neoplasms due to malignant potential
recommendations.notes: Surgery for >3 cm with suspicious features

recommendations.recommendation: Use Bosniak classification for renal incidentaloma management
Obtain previous imaging to assess lesion stability when available
recommendations.notes: May obviate need for further workup

recommendations.recommendation: Consider patient health, life expectancy, and wishes in workup decisions
Engage in shared decision making with the patient with emphasis on the patient's values while ensuring understanding of risks of further imaging and interventions
recommendations.notes: Fundamental principle for managing all incidentalomas

recommendations.recommendation: Follow ACR Incidental Findings Committee guidelines for managing incidental findings on abdominal CT
Use evidence-based endocrinology principles for management of pituitary incidentalomas
recommendations.notes: Follow Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline

recommendations.recommendation: Follow American Thyroid Association management guidelines for thyroid nodules
recommendations.notes: Revised guidelines for thyroid nodules and differentiated thyroid cancer

recommendations.recommendation: Use ACCP evidence-based clinical practice guidelines for evaluation of pulmonary nodules
recommendations.notes: Addresses when pulmonary nodule evaluation indicates lung cancer

red_flags: Pituitary lesion ≥1 cm with solid component or optic nerve impingement
Vision changes or signs of hormonal hypersecretion
History of neck radiation or family history of thyroid malignancy
Pulmonary nodule with history of malignancy
Irregular borders, eccentric calcifications, or low density in pulmonary nodules
Hepatic lesion with ill-defined margins or enhancement >20 HU
Pancreatic cyst >3 cm with mural nodules or lymphadenopathy
Adrenal lesion >4 cm or high attenuation >10 HU
Bosniak category IIF or greater renal lesions
Ovarian lesions with thickened walls/septa or solid components with blood flow
Elevated CA-125 in postmenopausal women with ovarian lesions
extraction.follow_up: Variable by organ system: Pituitary MRI in 6-12 months depending on size, thyroid ultrasonography in 6 months, pulmonary nodules over 6-24 months (3 months for high-risk), hepatic lesions with serial imaging, pancreatic cysts with radiologic monitoring for 1-3 cm lesions, adrenal lesions monitored for stability over 6-12 months, renal category IIF lesions followed with imaging, ovarian cysts monitored 6-12 weeks to yearly depending on characteristics
extraction.escalation_path: Refer for surgery: pancreatic cysts >3 cm with suspicious features, adrenal lesions >4 cm (usually biopsied), renal category III/IV lesions, complex ovarian lesions. Biopsy considerations: thyroid nodules >1-2 cm, adrenal lesions >4 cm, hepatic lesions when hepatocellular carcinoma cannot be excluded. Subspecialty consultation: endocrinology for pituitary hormone assessment, surgery for concerning lesions requiring resection, radiology consultation for complex imaging interpretation
